title: NextNav Inc. Petition for Rulemaking — Reconfiguration of Lower 900 MHz Band (902-928 MHz)
description:
NextNav (formerly Progeny, M-LMS licensee) petitions FCC to reconfigure 902-928 MHz band. Proposes 5 MHz uplink (902-907) paired with 10 MHz downlink (918-928) for flexible-use 5G PNT network complementing GPS. All other users compressed into 907-918 MHz (11 MHz). NextNav seeks to swap its M-LMS spectrum holdings for single nationwide 15 MHz flexible-use license.

Create new band plan: 902-907 MHz (5 MHz uplink), 907-918 MHz (compressed ISM/Part 15/amateur), 918-928 MHz (10 MHz downlink). Convert M-LMS to flexible-use licensing. Would eliminate Part 15 operations in 15 of 26 MHz of current ISM band. Millions of deployed IoT devices (LoRa, Zigbee, smart meters, RFID) would be impacted. Amateur 33cm band severely reduced.
status: Comments received, under FCC review — no NPRM issued yet

docket_number: WT Docket No. 20-443 / GN Docket No. 22-352
title: Expanding Flexible Use of the 12.2-12.7 GHz Band
description:
FCC adopted rules preserving 12.2-12.7 GHz for satellite services (DBS and NGSO like Starlink), rejecting Dish Network push for two-way 5G mobile broadband in the band. FCC granted SpaceX Earth Stations in Motion (ESIM) authority for Starlink on moving vehicles, boats, aircraft. January 2026: FCC granted SpaceX time-limited waiver to exceed certain power limits for Starlink Gen2, contingent on ongoing rulemaking outcome.

Original Dish proposal: reallocate for two-way 5G terrestrial mobile — REJECTED. Current proceeding: revisiting NGSO-GSO sharing regime in 10.7-12.7, 17.3-18.6, and 19.7-20.2 GHz. SpaceX seeks increased power limits. GSO operators (SES, Intelsat) oppose SpaceX power increases citing interference to legacy geostationary satellites.
status: R&O adopted preserving satellite use; ongoing NGSO-GSO sharing rulemaking

title: Upper C-Band (3.98-4.2 GHz) NPRM
description:
FCC NPRM to repurpose Upper C-band for licensed commercial use. Part of OBBBA mandate requiring at least 100 MHz auction by July 2027. Band currently used by fixed satellite service (FSS) earth stations for satellite downlinks. Would extend C-band commercial deployment above current 3.7-3.98 GHz CBRS/5G allocation.

Reallocate up to 220 MHz (3.98-4.2 GHz) from satellite downlink to terrestrial mobile broadband. Transition plan for incumbent FSS earth stations. Auction of at least 100 MHz required within 2 years of OBBBA enactment. Satellite operators seeking compensation similar to C-band transition (3.7 GHz clearing).
status: NPRM adopted October 2025, comments period open

title: Lower 3 GHz Band (3.1-3.45 GHz) Sharing Study
description:
NTIA and DoD studying whether 350 MHz of S-band spectrum can be shared with commercial users. DoD determined sharing technically feasible with advanced interference mitigation and coordination framework. Band used by Navy shipborne radar (SPN-43, SPQ-9B) and ground-based air defense. OBBBA explicitly excluded this band from mandatory reallocation pipeline, protecting DoD access.

Potential dynamic spectrum sharing framework similar to CBRS. Would need Environmental Sensing Capability (ESC) or equivalent coordination system. Sen. Rounds blocked reconciliation without exemption for this band and 6 GHz. Commercial 5G operators want access to contiguous mid-band spectrum to rival China/Korea. Study examining coexistence, frequency compression, and access below 3.1 GHz.
status: Under NTIA/DoD study, final report due October 2026. Excluded from OBBBA mandatory reallocation.

docket_number: H.R. 1 / One Big Beautiful Bill Act
title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act — 800 MHz Spectrum Pipeline and FCC Auction Authority Restoration
description:
Restores FCC general spectrum auction authority through September 30, 2034 (lapsed March 9, 2023). Directs FCC to auction at least 800 MHz of spectrum including 100+ MHz of Upper C-band within 2 years. NTIA must identify 800 MHz of spectrum between 1.3-10 GHz for non-federal 5G use within 2 years. Excludes 3.1-3.45 GHz and 7.4-8.4 GHz from mandatory reallocation. CBO estimates $85B in auction revenue over 10 years.

NTIA identified first bands for study: 1.675-1.680 GHz (NOAA MetSat), plus four additional bands: 1.680-1.695 GHz, 2.7-2.9 GHz, 4.4-4.94 GHz, 7.125-7.4 GHz. FCC must conduct first auction within 2 years. Multiple auctions expected through 2034. Exempts 5.9-7.1 GHz (6 GHz unlicensed) from reallocation.
status: Signed into law July 4, 2025. Implementation underway.

title: Unlicensed Use of the 6 GHz Band — VLP Expansion to Full Band
description:
FCC expanded Very Low Power (VLP) device operation from UNII-5/UNII-7 only to the entire 6 GHz band (all four UNII sub-bands 5-8). VLP devices can operate across full 1200 MHz without AFC. Same power levels as existing VLP rules (14 dBm EIRP max, -8 dBm/MHz PSD). Enables wearables, AR/VR, short-range IoT throughout 6 GHz.

Expands VLP from UNII-5 (5.925-6.425) and UNII-7 (6.525-6.875) to include UNII-6 (6.425-6.525) and UNII-8 (6.875-7.125). No change to LPI or Standard Power rules. AFC still required for standard power outdoor. Maintains protection of incumbent fixed microwave links.
status: Final Rule adopted January 2025, effective May 5, 2025

docket_number: IB Docket No. 22-271 / IB Docket No. 25-149
title: Satellite Spectrum Expansion — 12.7-13.25 GHz and Upper Microwave Bands
description:
FCC FNPRM seeks comment on expanding satellite use in 12.7-13.25 GHz by removing regulatory restrictions (potential removal of Table of Allocations Footnotes NG52 and NG57). Separate NPRM on upper microwave spectrum bands above 24 GHz for more intensive satellite use. Part of broader FCC effort to modernize satellite regulations (Part 25 to new Part 100 overhaul).

Remove or modify allocation footnotes restricting satellite operations in 12.7-13.25 GHz. New Part 100 rules replacing Part 25 for satellite licensing. Updated spectrum sharing framework between NGSO and GSO systems above 24 GHz. Streamlined licensing procedures for satellite constellations.
status: FNPRM/NPRM released May 27, 2025. Comments being gathered.

title: Space Modernization — Part 100 Satellite Licensing Overhaul
description:
FCC proposes comprehensive overhaul of satellite licensing rules. New Part 100 would replace decades-old Part 25 framework. Modernizes rules for the mega-constellation era (SpaceX Starlink, Amazon Kuiper, OneWeb). Addresses orbital debris, spectrum sharing between NGSO and GSO, streamlined processing. Most significant satellite regulatory reform in decades.

New Part 100 licensing framework. Updated orbital debris mitigation requirements. Revised NGSO-GSO sharing rules. Simplified application processing. New rules for large constellation management. Updated power flux density limits.

title: Revisiting NGSO-GSO Sharing Regime in Ku/Ka Bands
description:
FCC revisiting spectrum sharing rules between non-geostationary (NGSO) satellite systems like Starlink and traditional geostationary (GSO) satellites. SpaceX wants increased power limits for Starlink Gen2. GSO operators (SES, Intelsat) claim interference. FCC granted SpaceX time-limited waiver in January 2026 pending rulemaking outcome. March 2026: ongoing clash over power limits.

Updated EPFD (equivalent power flux density) limits for NGSO systems. Potential relaxation of power constraints that SpaceX argues are outdated for modern constellation designs. New interference coordination procedures. Balance between enabling next-gen NGSO and protecting legacy GSO investments.
status: NPRM initiated April 2025, SpaceX waiver granted January 2026

title: FCC Empowers Short-Range Radars in the 60 GHz Band
description:
Updated Part 15.255 rules for field disturbance sensors (FDS/radar) in 57-71 GHz band. Created tiered power/duty-cycle structure enabling in-vehicle occupant detection radar, gesture recognition, motion sensing. Maintained existing WiGig/WLAN communication rules unchanged. Balanced innovation in radar applications with coexistence with communication devices.

New tiered FDS power limits: 10 dBm (unrestricted), 20 dBm (indoor/50% duty cycle), 30 dBm (outdoor fixed in 57-59.4 GHz), 14 dBm (22.7% duty cycle). Removed previous restrictions that limited radar innovation. Enabled automotive in-cabin radar for child/pet detection, health monitoring.
status: Final Rule adopted May 2023, effective July 24, 2023

title: NTIA Identification of 1675-1680 MHz for Reallocation
description:
NTIA identified 1675-1680 MHz as its first band for reallocation under the OBBBA 800 MHz spectrum pipeline mandate. Band currently used by NOAA GOES Data Collection System (DCS) downlink at 1679.7-1680.1 MHz. Also disclosed four additional bands for study: 1.680-1.695 GHz, 2.7-2.9 GHz, 4.4-4.94 GHz, 7.125-7.4 GHz.

Potential reallocation of 5 MHz from meteorological satellite operations to commercial use. NOAA operations would need to be relocated or accommodated. Precedent set by AWS-3 transition of 1695-1710 MHz. Raises concerns about impact on weather forecasting data continuity.
status: Identified as first band for reallocation study under OBBBA pipeline

title: Transforming the 2.5 GHz Band
description:
FCC modernized 2.5 GHz band (EBS/BRS) rules. Tribal priority window allowed tribal entities to apply for unused spectrum on tribal lands. T-Mobile holds majority of existing BRS licenses and is largest user. Remaining unassigned spectrum to be auctioned. Band is critical mid-band 5G spectrum used by T-Mobile for nationwide coverage layer.

Overlay auction for unassigned EBS licenses. Geographic-based licensing replacing site-based. Updated buildout requirements. T-Mobile deployed 2.5 GHz for 5G nationwide.
status: Tribal priority window completed; overlay auction for remaining licenses pending

title: Automated Frequency Coordination (AFC) for 6 GHz Standard Power
description:
FCC approved AFC system operators to manage standard power outdoor operations in 6 GHz band. AFC databases contain locations of ~93,000 incumbent fixed microwave links. Standard power APs must query AFC before transmitting to get approved channels/power levels. Six AFC operators conditionally approved. System is operational but accuracy and update frequency remain areas of ongoing improvement.

AFC database maintenance and accuracy requirements. Coordination with fixed microwave incumbents. Potential future refinements to protection criteria. May inform spectrum sharing approaches for future bands (e.g., if 7.125-7.4 GHz is opened).
status: AFC systems conditionally approved and operational. Ongoing refinements.
